properties.field_incident_description: The Stoner Mesa Fire is OUT
properties.field_incident_overview:
The 2005 Stoner Mesa Fire burned along Stoner Creek and on Taylor Mesa. Fire behavior was extreme early in the incident due to the prolonged lack of moisture, low humidity levels, strong shifting winds and lots of available fuel. Heavy rainfall in late August and into September significantly reduced fire activity. The fire was called out December 3, 2025.

The Stoner Mesa Fire area closure has been rescinded. Dangerous conditions may persist from fire-weakened trees and dangerous conditions during heavy rain.
properties.title: Stoner Mesa Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: On Wednesday, November 5, 2025, a private landowner detected smoke south of Circleville, WV in Pendleton County. Pendleton County emergency personnel immediately contacted the USDA Forest Service who responded. This wildfire is being managed under unified command with the State of West Virginia and the USDA Forest Service, Monongahela National Forest.  Initial acreage was estimated at 50 acres, with active, wind driven fire. The fire is burning in an oak-hickory forest type, with very steep, mountainous terrain making ground access difficult. Safety of firefighters and the public are the top priorities.
properties.title: Woodside Fire

properties.field_incident_description: The Havasu Fire is an escaped prescribed fire, declared a wildfire at 4 p.m. on January 19th.
properties.field_incident_overview: The Havasu Fire is an escaped prescribed fire, declared a wildfire at 4 p.m. on January 19th.
properties.title: Havasu Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
On September 6, 2025, USFS fire managers were able to assess a new fire start caused by lightning, named the Tunnel Creek Fire, located in a remote area of the Buckhorn Wilderness in Olympic National Forest.

The fire is roughly 10 miles west of Quilcene, Washington and 1.5 miles west of the Tunnel Creek Trailhead accessed from FS Road 2740.

Updates related to the Tunnel Creek Fire will be shared on the Bear Gulch Fire Inciweb page as they are being managed by the same team.
properties.title: Tunnel Creek Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Cypress Creek Wilderness Fire in the Upland Island Wilderness Area started on Tuesday, February 24, 2026. The fire is located approximately 5.9 miles from Zavalla, TX in the Angelina National Forest.
properties.title: Cypress Creek Wilderness Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: At approximately 5 PM on Saturday, March 7, Texas A&M Forest Service was requested to assist local resources contain a large wildfire located approximately 7 miles southwest of Aspermont, Texas in Stonewall County. The fire in tall grass and brush experienced moderate fire behavior. Texas A&M Forest Service operated in unified command with local firefighters. The fire was fully contained just before midnight.
properties.title: Eagle Hill Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
WEBSITE ANNOUCEMENT: The Southern Area Incident Management Gray Team is no longer in command of the fire, and this website is no longer being monitored. Please see the official Big Cypress National Preserve website at www.nps.gov/BICY for updates on the fire including area closures. If you have additional questions, please contact the Big Cypress National Preserve duty officer at 561-923-5228.

The National Fire is burning within the Big Cypress National Preserve, approximately 25 miles east of Naples, Florida. It was discovered late on Sunday, February 22. The cause is unknown and under investigation. Initial attack efforts were unsuccessful due to rapid spread in difficult terrain. Local U.S. Wildland Fire Service fire managers transferred command of the fire to the Southern Area Gray Incident Management Team, led by Incident Commander Fulton Jeansonne, at 6:00 am on Thursday, February 26, 2026. On Monday, March 9th, 2026 command of the fire will be transferred back to the local unit. 

The fire is being managed under a full suppression strategy utilizing both direct and indirect tactics, including both ground firefighters and aerial resources. 

Area weather and environmental conditions are contributed to the difficulty of suppressing the National Fire. Factors include lack of rainfall, ongoing Stage 3 drought, recent frost kill of some vegetation, La Nina conditions, and multiple cold fronts with north winds.

The National Fire is burning in sawgrass prairies, pinelands, hardwood hammocks and cypress swamps.

SPECIAL NOTE: The National Fire location as shown on the main INCIWeb map contains an error. See the February 28 information map in the "maps" section for the current map. The fire is entirely east of SR-29. There is no fire west of SR-29.
properties.title: National

properties.field_incident_overview:
Evacuations: Curry County Sheriff John Ward has removed all evacation levels for the Moon Complex. 

The Emergency Area and Trail closure for the Moon Complex that went into effect November 14th of 2025, has been rescinded. Effective, February 24th, 2026, this closure was replaced with Emergency Trail Closure 06-10-28-26-04, which re-opens many of the camping areas along the lower Rogue River Corridor. While much of the fire-impacted area has been reopened, recreationists will continue to see effects from the fire and ongoing repair work in the area. 

For closure information, visit: https://www.fs.usda.gov/r06/rogue-siskiyou/alerts/rogue-river-national-recreation-trail-emergency-closure 

The remaining Rogue River Trail Closure begins at the western trailhead of the Rogue River Trail (Trail #1160) and follows the trail to the east for approximately 8 miles to Jackson Creek. The trail from Jackson Creek to Clay Hill Creek will be opened by May 1, 2026. The Forest will be assessing the segment from Clay Hill Creek to Foster Bar in the spring of 2026. 

On September 3, a lightning event on the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest caused a number of fires in the vicinity of the Rogue River about 11 miles northeast of Agness, Oregon. Six of these made up the initial Moon Complex: the Backbone, Brushy, Pinnacle, Tate, Paradise, and Stair fires. 

The Moon Complex is now in 100% containment status. 

Fire Restrictions: Tuesday, October 16, 2025, The Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest decreased the Fire Danger Rating to LOW across all five Ranger Districts and removed Public Use Restrictions forest-wide as well as on the Wild & Scenic section of the Rogue River. To learn more, visit the forest website: https://www.fs.usda.gov/alerts/rogue-siskiyou/alerts-notices

Fire Repair: Repair of fire line and roadways is the primary focus of resources engaged on the Moon Complex
properties.title: Moon Complex

properties.field_incident_overview: Shortly after 7 PM on Sunday, March 8, Texas A&M Forest Service was requested to assist local resources contain a large wildfire located approximately 3 miles north of Boys Ranch, Texas in Oldham County. The fire burned in thick brush and tall grass showing active fire behavior. Texas A&M Forest Service operated in unified command with local firefighters. Firefighters fully contained the fire at approximately 2 AM on Monday morning.
properties.title: Maroon Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: At approximately, 1 p.m. on Friday, March 6, 2025, firefighters and equipment from the Texas A&M Forest Service responded to a wildfire approximately 2 miles northeast of Matador, Texas. Local Fire Departments located the source of a large smoke plume and requested assistance. Brush trucks, engines and heavy equipment were used to fight the fire burning in grass, brush and timber. Aircraft was not needed.
properties.title: Ballard Creek Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: At approximately 4:43 PM on Monday, March 9, Texas A&M Forest Service was requested to assist local resources contain a large wildfire located approximately 2 miles east of Lefors, Texas in Gray County in the area of FM 291 and Highway 273. The fire was reported moving at a high rate of spread in dry grass and brush. The fire is being pushed by high winds from the southwest. Access is difficult due to very sandy soils and heavy brush conditions. Texas A&M Forest Service is operating in unified command with local volunteer fire departments. Road closures were in effect, but affected roads were reopened.Please visit Drive Texas for the latest road closure information.
properties.title: Cabin Creek Fire
